questions.q: Two forces compete inside a heavy nucleus like uranium. Which statement correctly describes them?
options: The strong nuclear force pushes protons apart; the electrostatic force binds nucleons over long range
The electrostatic force repels the protons; the strong nuclear force binds nucleons but acts only over a very short (nearest-neighbour) range
Gravity holds the nucleus together; the strong force tears it apart
Both forces are attractive and act equally over all distances
questions.explanation: The electrostatic (Coulomb) force repels every proton from every other across the whole nucleus, while the strong nuclear force is attractive but only reaches its nearest neighbours (~1 fm). In a large nucleus like uranium, the long-range repulsion nearly overwhelms the short-range binding, leaving it on the edge of stability.

questions.q: Why is a slow (thermal) neutron more effective than a fast neutron at inducing fission in U-235?
options: A slow neutron carries more energy to break the nucleus
A slow neutron has no charge and lingers near the nucleus long enough to be captured, giving a larger reaction cross-section
A fast neutron is repelled by the nucleus's positive charge
Slow neutrons decay into protons before striking the nucleus
questions.explanation: A neutron is uncharged, so it is not repelled; a slow neutron lingers near the nucleus long enough to be captured (a larger cross-section), making the target effectively bigger. A fast neutron tends to glance off. Speed is not needed to overcome repulsion because there is none.

questions.q: Where does the ~200 MeV of energy released in a single fission event actually come from?
options: From the kinetic energy of the incoming neutron
From a tiny mass defect: the products weigh slightly less than the reactants, and that lost mass becomes energy via \( E = mc^2 \)
From chemical bonds breaking inside the nucleus
From the electrostatic potential energy stored in the neutron
questions.explanation: The fission products together weigh slightly less than the original nucleus plus neutron. This mass defect is converted to energy by \(E = mc^2\); using \(1\ \mathrm{u} = 931.5\ \mathrm{MeV}\), a defect of ~0.186 u gives ~173 MeV (kinetic), and ~200 MeV total once gammas and fragment decay are included.

questions.q: On the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve (peaking near iron-56), why does splitting uranium release energy?
options: Uranium has the highest binding energy per nucleon of any element
The fission fragments lie higher up the curve (more tightly bound, closer to the iron peak) than uranium, so binding energy is released
The fragments have lower binding energy per nucleon than uranium
Iron is produced directly in every fission reaction
questions.explanation: Uranium sits on the right-hand slope at ~7.6 MeV/nucleon. Its fission fragments lie higher up, nearer the iron-56 peak (~8.5 MeV/nucleon), so they are more tightly bound. The surplus binding energy is released. Fission slides down the right of the curve toward iron; fusion climbs up the left — both head toward iron-56.

questions.q: A fission reaction releases a mass defect of 0.186 u. Using \(1\ \mathrm{u} = 931.5\ \mathrm{MeV}\), approximately how much energy is released?
options: 17 MeV
173 MeV
931 MeV
1.86 MeV
questions.explanation: \(E = \Delta m \times 931.5 = 0.186 \times 931.5 \approx 173\ \mathrm{MeV}\), carried mainly as kinetic energy of the fragments. Adding gamma rays and fragment decay brings the total to about 200 MeV per fission.

questions.q: The multiplication factor \(k\) is the average number of neutrons from each fission that cause a further fission. Which value corresponds to a steadily running power reactor?
options: \( k < 1 \) (subcritical)
\( k = 1 \) (critical)
\( k > 1 \) (supercritical)
questions.explanation: When \(k = 1\) (critical), each fission triggers exactly one more, so the chain sustains at a steady level — a controlled reactor. \(k < 1\) lets the chain die out; \(k > 1\) makes it grow exponentially, the runaway condition of a bomb.

questions.q: What is the role of a moderator in a fission reactor, and why is this counterintuitive?
options: It absorbs excess neutrons to slow the reaction; counterintuitive because absorbing neutrons speeds fission
It slows fast neutrons to thermal speeds; counterintuitive because slowing the neutrons down actually speeds up the chain reaction (slow neutrons cause fission far more readily)
It speeds neutrons up so they penetrate nuclei more easily
It cools the core by absorbing gamma rays
questions.explanation: Neutrons emerge fast, but slow neutrons cause fission far more readily. The moderator (light atoms like carbon or hydrogen) slows them by repeated collisions. The counterintuitive part: slowing the neutrons down actually increases the fission rate, because thermal neutrons have a much larger fission cross-section. Control rods (not the moderator) absorb excess neutrons.

questions.q: What is the purpose of control rods made of cadmium or boron in a reactor?
options: To slow down fast neutrons
To absorb spare neutrons, lowering the multiplication factor and controlling the reaction rate
To reflect neutrons back into the core
To carry heat away to the turbine
questions.explanation: Cadmium and boron are strong neutron absorbers. Inserting the rods soaks up spare neutrons and lowers \(k\); withdrawing them lets the reaction build. They are the reactor's throttle and brake. Slowing neutrons is the moderator's job; reflecting them is the reflector's.

questions.q: Why can a commercial power reactor (fuel ~3-5% U-235) NOT detonate like an atomic bomb (fuel >90% U-235)?
options: Reactors contain no fissile material at all
At low enrichment the chain cannot grow explosively fast — most neutrons are absorbed by non-fissile U-238 before a runaway can develop
Control rods make any detonation physically impossible regardless of fuel
Reactor uranium is a different element from bomb uranium
questions.explanation: A bomb needs the chain to grow explosively in about a millionth of a second, which requires highly enriched fuel (>90% U-235). At 3-5% enrichment most neutrons are captured by the abundant non-fissile U-238 before a runaway can build, so a reactor can melt down or suffer a steam explosion but cannot produce a nuclear detonation.

questions.q: Complete and check the reaction \( {}^{235}_{92}\mathrm{U} + {}^{1}_{0}n \to {}^{141}_{56}\mathrm{Ba} + {}^{A}_{Z}\mathrm{Kr} + 3\,{}^{1}_{0}n \). What are \(A\) and \(Z\) for the krypton nucleus?
questions.explanation: Conserve mass number: \(235 + 1 = 141 + A + 3(1)\), so \(A = 92\). Conserve atomic number: \(92 + 0 = 56 + Z + 3(0)\), so \(Z = 36\). The krypton nucleus is \({}^{92}_{36}\mathrm{Kr}\).
